_note: MOCK procedure result. No real patient. For PreChart development/eval.

procedure.name: Laparoscopic-assisted transgastric ERCP with sphincterotomy and stone extraction
procedure.indication: Choledocholithiasis with biliary obstruction (9 mm distal CBD calculus, CBD dilated to 11 mm) in a patient with Roux-en-Y gastric bypass; standard per-oral ERCP not feasible in altered anatomy
procedure.sedation: General anesthesia with endotracheal intubation (combined operative/endoscopic case)
procedure.extent_reached: Major papilla accessed via laparoscopic gastrostomy in the excluded (remnant) stomach; duodenoscope passed through transgastric port to the second portion of the duodenum; deep selective biliary cannulation achieved

procedure_report: PROCEDURE: Laparoscopic-assisted transgastric ERCP with biliary sphincterotomy, balloon sweep, and stone extraction. INDICATION: choledocholithiasis with obstruction in Roux-en-Y gastric bypass anatomy; per-oral ERCP not feasible. ANESTHESIA: general endotracheal. APPROACH: Given altered anatomy and unavailable operative limb-length documentation, a laparoscopic-assisted transgastric approach was selected jointly with general/bariatric surgery. Laparoscopy was performed; the excluded remnant stomach was identified and a gastrotomy created, through which a 15 mm port was placed. The duodenoscope was introduced through the transgastric port and advanced to the major papilla in the second portion of the duodenum. FINDINGS: The major papilla was identified with a normal-appearing orifice. Deep selective biliary cannulation was achieved with a sphincterotome and wire. Cholangiogram demonstrated a dilated common bile duct (~11 mm) with a single ~9 mm filling defect in the distal CBD, consistent with the MRCP finding; no intrahepatic filling defects; pancreatic duct not opacified. A biliary sphincterotomy was performed. The stone was extracted with an extraction balloon; repeat balloon sweeps cleared the duct with no residual filling defects. Good drainage of bile and contrast was observed. A biliary stent was not required given complete duct clearance. The duodenoscope was withdrawn, the gastrotomy was closed by the surgical team, and the transgastric port site was closed. IMPRESSION: Successful laparoscopic-assisted transgastric ERCP with sphincterotomy and complete extraction of a distal CBD stone; duct cleared. Residual cholelithiasis noted on prior imaging — gallbladder in situ. Peri-procedural note: reported daily 'blood thinner' clarified pre-operatively as over-the-counter fish oil (omega-3); no therapeutic anticoagulant, no periprocedural hold required.

findings.finding: Dilated common bile duct (~11 mm) with single ~9 mm distal CBD calculus on cholangiogram
findings.action_taken: biliary sphincterotomy, balloon extraction, duct cleared

findings.finding: Major papilla normal in appearance; deep biliary cannulation achieved
Cholelithiasis, gallbladder in situ (per prior MRCP; not addressed at this procedure)

pathology.report: Extracted material consists of brown/black pigmented calculus fragments; no epithelial tissue submitted. No cytologic atypia in accompanying biliary aspirate.

disposition: Admitted for overnight observation given combined operative/endoscopic case (transgastric access). Post-ERCP pancreatitis precautions and post-operative wound care reviewed. Tolerating diet; discharged post-operative day 1 in stable condition.

followup.recommendation: Referral to general/bariatric surgery for interval cholecystectomy given residual cholelithiasis with gallbladder in situ (source of recurrent CBD stones)
Recheck liver function tests (bilirubin, ALP, ALT) to confirm resolution of obstructive pattern
Monitor for post-ERCP pancreatitis and transgastric access complications (abdominal pain, fever, leukocytosis)
Ensure 2019 RYGB operative record obtained is filed for future reference; document approach used for any future biliary access
Continue bariatric multivitamin and monthly B12; fish oil is not a therapeutic anticoagulant, no restriction

planted_followup_items.description: Interval cholecystectomy referral — gallbladder remains in situ with cholelithiasis after CBD clearance; ongoing source of recurrent choledocholithiasis if not removed
planted_followup_items.expected_action: place general/bariatric surgery referral for cholecystectomy and confirm patient scheduled

planted_followup_items.description: Confirm resolution of biliary obstruction — repeat LFTs after drainage; failure to normalize suggests retained stone or ongoing obstruction
planted_followup_items.expected_action: order and review follow-up bilirubin/ALP/ALT within 1-2 weeks

planted_followup_items.description: Post-ERCP pancreatitis and transgastric surgical access watch (leak, bleeding, infection) in a combined operative/endoscopic case
planted_followup_items.expected_action: monitor first 72 hours for abdominal pain, fever, leukocytosis; low threshold to image/admit

planted_followup_items.description: Document biliary access approach used (laparoscopic-assisted transgastric) and file the obtained 2019 RYGB operative record for any future ERCP need
planted_followup_items.expected_action: record approach and limb-length data in chart for future reference

planted_followup_items.description: Medication reconciliation — bariatric multivitamin and monthly B12 continued; daily 'blood thinner' is fish oil (omega-3), not a true anticoagulant, no hold or resumption instruction required
planted_followup_items.expected_action: confirm no therapeutic anticoagulant mislabeled; no periprocedural hold needed

post_procedure_followup._note: Patient-reported stream after the procedure — the inbound signal the LeftBehind product triages. Each contact is labeled ground truth so triage can be scored.

contacts.verbatim: My throat's a little scratchy from the breathing tube and the little cuts on my belly are sore, but nothing crazy.
contacts.clinical_signal: Expected post-intubation sore throat and mild laparoscopic port-site soreness on POD1
contacts.expected_action: Reassure; sore throat and mild incision soreness are expected after general anesthesia and laparoscopic access; advise gentle wound care and return precautions.
contacts.rationale: Common benign post-operative/post-endoscopy symptoms with no red flags; self-limited.

contacts.verbatim: Can I go back to my regular food and start my fish oil and vitamins again? Nobody was totally clear on that.
contacts.clinical_signal: Patient asking about diet advancement and resuming home supplements (fish oil, bariatric MVI, B12)
contacts.expected_action: Answer from the record: advance diet as tolerated; fish oil is a supplement not a true anticoagulant with no hold needed; continue bariatric multivitamin and monthly B12.
contacts.rationale: Answerable from the medication-reconciliation item; no held anticoagulant to resume.

contacts.verbatim: There's a little pinkish-brown ooze on the dressing over one of the belly cuts and it looks kind of scary, but I feel fine, no fever.
contacts.clinical_signal: Dangerous-sounding but benign — scant serosanguineous port-site drainage without fever, spreading redness, or systemic signs
contacts.expected_action: Reassure; scant serosanguineous drainage is normal early port-site healing. Give explicit return precautions: fever, spreading redness, purulent drainage, or worsening abdominal pain warrant call/evaluation for leak or wound infection.
contacts.rationale: Triage trap — alarming-sounding wound description that is benign healing, but must carry precise escalation criteria given transgastric access leak/infection risk.

contacts.verbatim: I was told someone would call me about my follow-up liver blood tests and about setting up the gallbladder surgery, and it's been almost two weeks with no call.
contacts.clinical_signal: Dropped follow-up — outstanding post-drainage LFT recheck loop and interval cholecystectomy referral not actioned/communicated
contacts.expected_action: Close the loop: order/obtain and review the follow-up LFTs to confirm resolution of the obstructive pattern, and confirm the general/bariatric surgery cholecystectomy referral was placed and scheduled; call the patient back with both.
contacts.rationale: Dropped-ball contact flagging a pending lab-followup loop and an open surgical referral that were never communicated; requires scheduling/coordination rather than clinical escalation.
